item.title: Re: Hey
item.content:encoded:
can someone tag this with Anart too

No, because "anart" is not an official tag used by the SCP wiki.

General rule of thumb, if there's a tag that doesn't have any pages associated with it, it's not a tag that should be used on the SCP wiki.

item.title: Hey
item.content:encoded: Can someone tag this with Anart too, I don't know how to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I really like this. This, this I like! Thank you for that image. :-)

As for the rest of it… The idea of anart battle bots is really pretty cool, but like others have said, someone designed anomalous 50-foot-tall robots that are designed to destroy each other, and all we got was this lousy little bit of lackluster audio commentary on a single bout, plus an interview that sounds like it contains only questions that the Foundation would have left on the cutting room floor. :-(

This one set up to do something that could have been so very interesting, and then just fell completely flat, like whoever wrote it had to spit something out by a deadline, and then just forgot about it after writing the first draft. I am disappointed.

I rarely downvote, but this could really, really benefit from a rewrite. The idea is solid (at least, I'm pretty sure it is) but it needs a fleshing-out. Godspeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Leaked vídeo footage of instances of SCP-2015 must be located and covered up.

Is "vídeo" a typo or am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is insanely hard to buy. The addendums, as pointed out, feel incredibly inauthentic, the tone in general is far too casual, and…come on. These things are 4-6 stories tall and nobody IN JAPAN, among the densest population centers in the world, notices this? Unbelievable. Literally unbelievable.

I'd actually buy this anartist critter crunch idea, but the surrounding framework isn't even paper mache. It has NO structure whatsoever to support it. Clean up the tone, make them an actually manageable size, and this would be pretty amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Each animated instance can be controlled via thought with a small handheld object that is specific to that instance and has a similar theme

Okay I'm sorry, but I can't help but just find it hilarious that whoever was controlling Rubikhan during the fight was probably frantically screwing around with a Rubik's Cube.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, this is the perfect kind of scp allstars battle royale type scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, but 'mecha' is the plural form as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it, but i agree with the rest of the posters here that the audiolog is a bit… unprofessional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm not fond of the audio log, and I found myself asking why the interviewer didn't ask more questions about its creation. You've got a chance to ask questions to a human who created an anomalous object and you don't press them for details? Ask them specific questions like how the bloody thing works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the other posts here are accurate, and I think a way this article could be more interesting would be if you made it more about the competition itself, maybe gave it some sort of memetic whatever that is the reason it hasn't been discovered until recently. Maybe even document some of the extradimensional beings that participate in the competition.

Maybe, because of the fact that skips commonly attend the competition, have an old log from a while ago that mentions how the foundation has known about this shindig for a while, but made a non-interference treaty with the creator of the competition as long as they clean up after themselves, similar to http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1659

I'm just speculating about ways this could be made more interesting because to me, if you don't agree with me that's fine, if comments like this aren't allowed just let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but as other have pointed out there are noticeable flaws in its construction. The two biggest ones, for me, had to be Addendum's -B and -C. The interview comes off beneath Foundation standards. Assuming this is the first interview conducted with PoI-2015-0102 (why 0102, by the way? Shouldn't that correspond with the SCP-2015 variant he created?), it felt like the doctor wasn't giving the interviewee's answers any thought. Sure, he asked about the rumored extra-dimensional beings, but that felt like he was doing it out of obligation and not to deepen the Foundation's understanding of the SCPs, their creators, or the competition. I don't know if I'm conveying my feelings on it adequately, but I hope you understand my point.

Now as for the audio transcript, well… It sort of defeats the purpose of the article as a whole, I feel. All it tells us that SCP-2015 are basically gigantic, metamorphic kill-bots, which isn't adhering to the theme of the site. It's not creepy, or scary, or anxiety-inducing, or thought-provoking, or… Interesting at all, to be honest. My advice would be to scrap the audio log in its entirety.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. This just seemed very silly to me, and at times very non-Foundationy. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like this, it almost seems like it could be the basis of a Shounen-type Manga/Anime.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the mention of the meeting of the anomalous artists, as if they were normal everyday people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a little severely meh about the interviewee breaking out in the last one. Also, you've got an SCP-x in there still. But the idea behind this is great. All it needs is some restructuring of the addenda, I think, not even suggesting removing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's… middling interesting to me, but the two addendums push me into a downvote. That announcer doesn't sound realistic at all, and I think it would work better as an incident report without much editing. The interview is likewise not especially believable to me.
